summary:
Oligomenorrhea indicates a potential issue related to disrupted endometrial function due to scarring or fibrosis within the uterus as observed in cases of Asherman Syndrome; thus it can serve as an important diagnostic clue for identifying this condition based upon abnormal bleeding patterns indicative thereof compared against typical physiological variations without such pathology present...
Ovarian hormones may not elicit appropriate feedback mechanisms resulting in irregularities like reduced frequency/magnitude of menses which further corroborates suspicion towards underlying structural abnormalities consistent only with specific etiologies associated directly through direct damage inflicted by prior procedures causing internal scar tissue formation inside reproductive tract cavities...

The summary focuses primarily on how oligomenorrhoea serves both clinically useful marker pointing at presence/possibility of AS while also noting broader context involving dysfunctional ovarian-hormonal interactions contributing overall picture suggestive but non-specific enough alone warrant definitive diagnosis requiring additional confirmatory tests/imaging etc..
